1. May Councilmember Gil discuss with neighborhood residents the potential
use of redevelopment to enhance the neighborhood where he owns property in tfre
neighborhood?

designation of a redevelopment area if that area has as its boundary the entire City of
Redlands, as opposed to just the neighborhood?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Yes. Nothing in the Act prevents Councilmenrber Gil from discussing issues
with residents of his neighborhood.

Step 4' WiIl Councilmember Gil's economic interests be directly or indirectly
involved in the decisions?
An economic interest that is directly involved in a governmental decision presents
a greater risk of a conflict of interest than does an economic interest that is indirectly
involved in the decision- Separate rules govern airrerent kinds of economic interest for
purposes of determining whether an economic interest is directly or indirectly involved.
A person, including business entities and sources of incomq is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or indirectly by
an agent:
"(r)-rnjtiates the proceeding in which the decision wil be
made by filing an application, claim, aptpeal,or similar
request or;
,,(2) Is a named party in,- or is the subject o{, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official,s
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision invorves the issuance, renewar, approvar, denial or
revocation of any ricense, permit, or otherentitlement to, or
"oltl"l with, the subject person.,, (Regulation
t870aJ@).)
Applying Regulation 18704.1(a) to the facts, if councilmember Gil or his tenants
do not initiate a proceeding in which a govemmental decision will be made and are not
named parties in or the subject ofproceedings before the city council, his rental business
and his tenants would be deemed L te irairlctly involv"J in tt "." 
governmental
decisions' (Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involvd in
goveflrmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regmded as indirectly involved.)
Real Property
Regulation 18704.2(a) sets forth six sets of circumstances under which real

deemed to be directly involved in a governmentar decision:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest,

Your facts suggest two alternative governmental desisions that the city council
may consider: (i) designation of a redevelopment survey area consisting primarily of the
North Redlands ay3a ard (ii) designation 9fa redevelopment survey area covering the
entire City of Redlands' Councilmernber Gil's properties would be directly involved in
both decisions under subdivision (5) because his properties are within the boundaries of
both potential redevelopment areas.
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
@egulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly o. i"air""tfy involved
in the agency's decision.
Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) applies to small businesses that are indirectly involved
in governmental decisions. It provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that it will result ii tel un
increase/decrease in the business entity's fiscal year gross revenues of $20,000 or more;
(B) the business "1firv incurring/avoiding additional[*penses or reducing/eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount $5,000 or more; or (c) an
increase/decrease in the value of the business entity's assets/liabitities of SZ0,000 or
With respect to Questions Two and Three, if the reasonably foreseeabie financial
effect on the business entity resulting from the redevelopment meets one of these
thresholds, it is material.
Regulation 18705-3 provides the materiality standard for an economic interest in
persons who are sources of income to Councilmember Gil. With respect to an individual
who is a source ofincome to an official and is indirectlyinvolved in a govemmental
decision, the financial effect is deerned material if any of tfr" followinglpplies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's incomg
investnents, or other tangibre or intangible assets or
liabilities (other than rear property) by $r,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision wilr affect the individual,s real property
interest in a manner that is considered material under Title

2, carifornia code of Regurations, Section rg705.2(b)j,
(Regutation I 8705.3 (bX3).)
Regulation 18705.2 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to real property
that is directly involved in a governmental decision. Subdivision (a)(l) provides that .,the
financial effect of a governmental decision on the real property i, pi"srrm"d to be
material' This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the r"J fiop"rty.,,
Accordingly, in order to rebut the presumption there must be ,o ,"*oruily foreseeable
financial effect, not even a penny'i worth, upon the offisial,s .eal propertr. t*,t,
provision is commonly referred to as the .bne penny ruIe.,,
Step 6' rs it reasonably foreseeabte that the financial effect of the govemmental
decisions on Councilmember Gil's economic interests will meet the"appticable
materiality standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is ..substantially
fikely." (Regulation rg706:' In re Thorn"i 1tsls1 I Fppc ops. l9g.) whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substintiauyiitery ui,rr" time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. (In re rTtorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered r"*orrubly
foreseeable' on the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is nJt reasonably
foreseeable. Each govemmental decision comingLefore councilmember Gil where he
wiff ue making, participating in making or influJncing the decision must be examined
individually to determine if it is reasonably foreseeabl that the decision will have a
material financial effect on his rental business, his tenants or his real property.
The determination of whether ot lgt it is reasonably foreseeable that aparticular
decision regarding redevelopment will affect councilmember Gil,s p.op"rty dy so much
as a penny's worth is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately fr; h# io decide.
An official's mere belief that there is no financial effect would not 6e *o"gt 1o rebut the
presumption of materiality. (Mc LaughlinAdvice Letter, No. A_M-l g2.)
You have not provided facts necessary for determining if decisions relating to the
1{welogment plan willforeseeablyhave a material financial effect on Councilmember
GiI's real property, rental business or sources of income. Since the goals oitt "
redevelopment plan proposed by the neishlorhood group are construction ofpublic

both the revenue of the rental business and the value of the property located within the
redevelopment area will be affected. (In re oglesby (1975) I Fppi ops. zr.j

Section 87lol (Regulation 18708) provides an exception under which a public

An economic interest that is directly involved in a governmental decision presents
a greater risk of a conflict of interest than does an economic interest that is indirectly
involved in the decision- Separate rules govern airrerent kinds of economic interest for
purposes of determining whether an economic interest is directly or indirectly involved.

Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
@egulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly o. i"air""tfy involved
in the agency's decision.
Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) applies to small businesses that are indirectly involved
in governmental decisions. It provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that it will result ii tel un
increase/decrease in the business entity's fiscal year gross revenues of $20,000 or more;

With respect to Questions Two and Three, if the reasonably foreseeabie financial

Regulation 18705-3 provides the materiality standard for an economic interest in

use of redevelopment to enhance the neighborhood where he owns property in tfre
neighborhood?
2. May he initiate and participate in a dialogue with the city council on a
potential redevelopment proj ect within the neighborhoorl?
3. May he participate in discussions with the cilr council and vote upon the
designation of a redevelopment area if that area has as its boundary the entire City of
Redlands, as opposed to just the neighborhood?

with residents of his neighborhood.
